main_actor: Sargon II of Assyria
description: After a three-year siege begun under Shalmaneser V, the Assyrian army under Sargon II took the capital Samaria and dissolved the northern Kingdom of Israel. According to Sargon's Khorsabad annals the Assyrians deported 27,290 Israelites, resettling them in Halah, the Habor region, and the cities of the Medes. Foreign populations were in turn resettled in Samaria — the origin of the later Samaritan community. The deported tribes are the source of the 'Ten Lost Tribes' tradition.
casualties_source: Sargon II Khorsabad annals, line 23 (27,290 exiles); modern estimates incl. war dead 50k-80k
ethical_notes: The deportation is documented both in Assyrian royal inscriptions (imperial viewpoint — presented as triumph) and in the Hebrew Bible (2 Kings 17 — presented as divine punishment). The Samaritan community's continuous presence in the region is often erased in narratives treating the deportation as total; it was not. The 'Ten Lost Tribes' tradition has been misused in modern racialist pseudo-history (British Israelism, identity movements) and AtlasPI records the event as documented demographic displacement, not metaphysical disappearance.

sources.citation: Sargon II, Khorsabad Annals (primary, ~710 BCE).
Tanakh, 2 Kings 17:5-6, 17:24-41 (primary).
Younger, K.L. (1998). 'The Deportations of the Israelites.' Journal of Biblical Literature 117/2.
Na'aman, N. (1993). 'Population Changes in Palestine Following Assyrian Deportations.' Tel Aviv 20.

main_actor: Sennacherib of Assyria
description: In reprisal for persistent Babylonian revolts and for the murder of his son Aššur-nādin-šumi, Sennacherib stormed Babylon, broke its walls, flooded the city by diverting the Arahtu canal, and had the statue of Marduk carried off to Assur. His own royal inscription (the 'Bavian inscription') claims he razed the city completely — an unusual admission of destruction of a sacred royal capital. The sack provoked lasting religious scandal in Mesopotamia and Sennacherib was later murdered by two of his sons; his successor Esarhaddon undertook a penitential rebuilding of Babylon.
ethical_notes: Sennacherib's destruction of Babylon is one of the rare ancient cases in which the perpetrator boasts of demolishing a sacred city of his own cultural sphere, then faces internal Assyrian religious backlash. Brinkman and Grayson both note that later Assyrian historiography (Esarhaddon's apology) partly suppresses the violence by framing it as theological error rather than political massacre.

sources.citation: Sennacherib, Bavian Inscription (primary, ~688 BCE).
Brinkman, J.A. (1984). Prelude to Empire: Babylonian Society and Politics 747-626 B.C. Occasional Publications of the Babylonian Fund 7.
Grayson, A.K. (1975). Assyrian and Babylonian Chronicles. Texts from Cuneiform Sources V.

main_actor: Cyaxares of Media and Nabopolassar of Babylon
description: An alliance of Medes under Cyaxares and Babylonians under Nabopolassar besieged and destroyed Nineveh, the Assyrian capital. The Tigris flooded and breached a section of wall; the allied armies stormed the breach, burned the royal palaces, massacred much of the population, and looted the city. The Book of the Prophet Nahum — contemporary — celebrates the fall. With Nineveh's destruction the Neo-Assyrian Empire, which had dominated the Near East for three centuries, effectively collapsed; a remnant under Aššur-uballiṭ II held out at Harran until 609 BCE.
casualties_source: Babylonian Chronicle (ABC 3, Fall of Nineveh Chronicle) + modern estimates
ethical_notes: Assyrian state violence (mass deportations, impalements, skin-flaying recorded in royal art) produced a retributive memory; the fall of Nineveh was treated in contemporary Hebrew prophetic literature (Nahum) as divine justice. Modern historiography documents both the cruelty of Assyrian imperial practice and the destruction of irreplaceable cuneiform archives and royal art in the sack.

sources.citation: Babylonian Chronicle ABC 3 (Fall of Nineveh Chronicle), British Museum BM 21901 (primary).
Tanakh, Book of Nahum (primary).
Stronach, D. (1997). 'Notes on the Fall of Nineveh.' in Assyria 1995.

main_actor: Nebuchadnezzar II of Babylon
description: After an 18-month siege, Nebuchadnezzar II's army breached Jerusalem on the 9th of Av. The First Temple, built by Solomon, was burned; the city walls razed; King Zedekiah captured, his sons killed before him, he was then blinded and taken in chains to Babylon. The Babylonian Chronicles and the biblical books of 2 Kings, Jeremiah, and Lamentations converge on the event. Much of the political and priestly elite was deported to Babylon — the 'Babylonian Captivity' — which reshaped Judean religion (synagogue worship, canonisation of Torah, diaspora consciousness).
ethical_notes: The 586 BCE destruction is one of the most thoroughly documented ancient conquests (Babylonian Chronicle ABC 5, archaeological burn layer at Jerusalem, Lachish ostraca, prophetic books). The deportation did not depopulate Judah — archaeological data show continued rural settlement — but the elite deportation decapitated state institutions. The event is commemorated annually on the 9th of Av (Tisha B'Av) and is foundational for Jewish diasporic identity.

sources.citation: Babylonian Chronicle ABC 5 (Chronicle of Nebuchadnezzar), British Museum BM 21946 (primary).
Tanakh, 2 Kings 24-25; Jeremiah 52; Lamentations (primary).
Lipschits, O. (2005). The Fall and Rise of Jerusalem: Judah Under Babylonian Rule. Eisenbrauns.

main_actor: Cyrus II ('the Great') of Persia
description: After the Battle of Opis, Cyrus II's army entered Babylon on the 16th of Tashritu (12 October 539 BCE), reportedly without a fight — the Nabonidus Chronicle records that 'the army of Cyrus entered Babylon without battle' after the defection of Gobryas, governor of Gutium. The Cyrus Cylinder records Cyrus's policy of restoring deported cult statues to their home cities and permitting displaced populations to return. In biblical tradition (Ezra 1, Isaiah 45) this included the edict allowing exiled Judeans to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple. The event ends the Neo-Babylonian Empire and inaugurates Achaemenid Persian hegemony over the Near East.
ethical_notes: The Cyrus Cylinder has been read anachronistically as a 'first charter of human rights' (1971 Iranian imperial propaganda, UN pedestal installation). Academic consensus (Kuhrt, Brosius) treats it as a conventional Mesopotamian royal foundation text framing conquest in the language of cultic restoration; the policies it describes (allowing exiles to return) are real and significant, but the modern human-rights reading imports 20th-century categories. The liberation of Judean exiles is documented in multiple independent sources.

entity_links.notes: Judean exiles permitted to return (Ezra 1, Cyrus Cylinder)

sources.citation: Nabonidus Chronicle (ABC 7), British Museum BM 35382 (primary).
Cyrus Cylinder, British Museum BM 90920 (primary, ~539 BCE).
Tanakh, Ezra 1; Isaiah 44-45 (primary).
Kuhrt, A. (2007). The Persian Empire: A Corpus of Sources. Routledge.

main_actor: Cambyses II of Persia
description: Cambyses II defeated the Egyptian army of Psamtik III at the Battle of Pelusium and captured Memphis, ending the Saite (26th) Dynasty and incorporating Egypt as the Achaemenid satrapy of Mudraya. Egyptian priestly sources (the Udjahorresnet statue inscription) record Cambyses adopting pharaonic titulature; Herodotus (Histories III) reports atrocities — the killing of the Apis bull, parody of Egyptian religious ritual — which modern scholarship treats as partly polemical. Egypt would remain under Persian rule, with one intermission (404-343 BCE), until Alexander's conquest in 332 BCE.
casualties_source: Herodotus III.10-13 (Egyptian and Persian dead); modern estimates speculative
ethical_notes: Herodotus's account of Cambyses's sacrileges and madness has been largely rejected by modern Egyptology: the Udjahorresnet inscription and Serapeum stelae show Cambyses performing proper pharaonic burial rites for the Apis bull. Anti-Cambyses tradition likely originated in Egyptian priestly hostility to reduced temple endowments and was then amplified in Greek anti-Persian historiography. The conquest itself and the harsh fiscal pressure are genuine; the cartoon cruelty is largely polemical.

sources.citation: Udjahorresnet naophorous statue, Vatican Museum 22690 (primary).
Herodotus, Histories III.1-38 (primary, ~430 BCE).
Lloyd, A.B. (1988). 'Herodotus on Cambyses: Some Thoughts on Recent Work.' in Achaemenid History III.

main_actor: Pericles of Athens, architects Iktinos and Kallikrates, sculptor Pheidias
description: Construction of the Parthenon, a Doric peripteral temple to Athena Parthenos, on the Athenian Acropolis. The project was financed in significant part from the tribute of the Delian League — that is, from the contributions of Athens's imperial allies, a point made explicit by Pericles's political opponents (Plutarch, Pericles 12). Pheidias's chryselephantine statue of Athena Parthenos (~12 m tall) and the sculptural programme of the pediments, metopes, and frieze set the canonical standard of Greek classical sculpture. The Parthenon Marbles — removed by Lord Elgin 1801-05 and held by the British Museum — are the subject of a continuing Greek restitution claim.
ethical_notes: The Periclean building programme was not purely cultural: Plutarch preserves the contemporary charge that Pericles was spending allied tribute as if it were Athenian revenue. Treating the Parthenon only as an aesthetic milestone without noting the imperial-tributary financing edits out a central fact of Athenian 5th-century politics. The continuing British retention of the Parthenon Marbles is a live restitution issue and should be recorded as such, not as a neutral 'British Museum holding'.

entity_links.notes: Monumental programme of the Periclean age

sources.citation: Plutarch, Life of Pericles 12-14 (primary, ~100 CE).
Thucydides, Histories II.13 (primary, ~410 BCE).
Hurwit, J.M. (1999). The Athenian Acropolis. Cambridge UP.

main_actor: Athenian demos (jury of 500)
description: Socrates was tried and executed by the restored Athenian democracy on charges of impiety (asebeia) and corrupting the young. The jury voted roughly 280-220 to convict and 360-140 to impose the death penalty. Socrates drank hemlock in the state prison. The trial took place in a city traumatised by defeat in the Peloponnesian War and the oligarchic Thirty Tyrants regime of 404-403 BCE, several of whose leaders (Critias, Charmides) had been Socrates's associates. The trial is documented by Socrates's own pupils Plato (Apology, Crito, Phaedo) and Xenophon (Apology, Memorabilia).
ethical_notes: Popular reception (Enlightenment through to modern textbooks) casts the trial as democracy murdering a saint of reason. Recent scholarship (Stone, Cartledge, Waterfield) contextualises the political charges: Socrates's circle included participants in the Thirty's terror, and the post-war amnesty explicitly forbade prosecution for political crimes — the religious charge was a legal proxy. The execution still marks a real limit of democratic tolerance for philosophical dissent; both readings coexist.

sources.citation: Plato, Apology, Crito, Phaedo (primary, ~390 BCE).
Xenophon, Apology; Memorabilia I.1-2 (primary, ~370 BCE).
Waterfield, R. (2009). Why Socrates Died: Dispelling the Myths. Norton.

main_actor: Alexander III of Macedon and Darius III of Persia
description: On 1 October 331 BCE Alexander III defeated the full imperial army of Darius III on a prepared battlefield east of the Tigris. Alexander fielded ~47,000 Macedonian and allied troops; Persian numbers are reported as 200,000-1,000,000 by ancient sources (Arrian, Diodorus) and estimated at 50,000-120,000 by modern historians. The Macedonian oblique-line manoeuvre created a gap through which the Companion cavalry drove at Darius's centre; Darius fled a second time. The victory opened the Achaemenid imperial heartlands (Babylon, Susa, Persepolis, Ecbatana) and ended Achaemenid sovereignty in practice, though Darius was not killed until 330 BCE by the satrap Bessus.
casualties_source: Arrian III.15 (Persian ~300k, Macedonian ~500 — both partisan); Engels (1978) modern estimates
ethical_notes: Ancient battle casualty figures are systematically inflated on the losing side and minimised on the winning side; Arrian and Diodorus depend on the Macedonian court historian Callisthenes and on Ptolemy's memoirs. Modern estimates still place Persian losses an order of magnitude above Macedonian losses, but the near-zero Macedonian casualties of ancient sources are implausible. The subsequent conquest of the Achaemenid empire involved mass enslavements, the burning of Persepolis (-330 BCE), and the murder of Darius III by his own subordinate — the transition was violent, not a triumphal parade.

sources.citation: Arrian, Anabasis Alexandri III.8-15 (primary, ~145 CE).
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica XVII.53-61 (primary, ~30 BCE).
Engels, D.W. (1978). Alexander the Great and the Logistics of the Macedonian Army. UC Press.

main_actor: Alexander III of Macedon
description: Alexander III of Macedon died at Babylon on 10-11 June 323 BCE at age 32, after a fever that lasted roughly twelve days. Medical hypotheses range from malaria to typhoid to alcohol-related pancreatitis; deliberate poisoning (by the Antipatrid faction) was alleged in antiquity and is not fully excluded by modern scholars but lacks decisive evidence. Because he left no designated adult heir, the empire fragmented into the Wars of the Diadochi (323-281 BCE): Ptolemy took Egypt, Seleucus took Mesopotamia and further east, Antigonus and later the Antigonids took Macedon, Lysimachus took Thrace. The Hellenistic kingdoms would shape the eastern Mediterranean and western Asia for three centuries.
casualties_source: Royal Ephemerides (ancient court diary, excerpted in Plutarch and Arrian)
ethical_notes: Ancient Greek and Roman historiography treated Alexander's reign as a civilising force; Persian, Zoroastrian, and later Iranian traditions preserved a counter-memory of him as 'Alexander the Accursed' (Gujastak Alexander in the Arda Viraf text) for the burning of Persepolis and the destruction of Zoroastrian Avestan manuscripts. Both readings are legitimate historical memory. His death without succession triggered roughly 40 years of continuous Hellenistic civil war with casualty totals in the hundreds of thousands.

entity_links.notes: Macedonian empire fragments into Diadochi successor states

sources.citation: Plutarch, Life of Alexander 75-77 (primary, ~100 CE).
Arrian, Anabasis Alexandri VII.25-28 (primary, ~145 CE).
Bosworth, A.B. (2002). The Legacy of Alexander: Politics, Warfare, and Propaganda under the Successors. Oxford UP.

main_actor: Mauryan emperor Aśoka
description: Following his extraordinarily bloody conquest of Kalinga (~262-261 BCE), Aśoka recorded in his Thirteenth Major Rock Edict that 100,000 Kalingans were killed, many more died of famine and disease, and 150,000 were deported — and that the scale of the suffering caused him to embrace Buddhist dharma and renounce conquest by force. He sent diplomatic-religious missions as far as the Hellenistic courts of Antiochus II, Ptolemy II, Antigonus Gonatas, Magas of Cyrene, and Alexander of Epirus. His edicts — carved on rocks and polished pillars across the subcontinent — are the earliest substantial corpus of imperial proclamations in South Asia. The conversion did not dismantle the Mauryan state but reframed its ideology.
casualties_source: Aśoka, Thirteenth Major Rock Edict (self-reported figures — see ethical_notes)
ethical_notes: The Kalinga figures come from Aśoka's own inscription, which makes them unusually credible as an admission of royal violence — kings in antiquity rarely boasted of their own cruelty. But the narrative of the remorseful convert-king was also a propagandistic framing useful to legitimise a non-expansionist late Mauryan state. Thapar (1961, 1997) argues the conversion was gradual rather than an event, and the Kalinga edict itself notes that Aśoka still retained royal force against forest peoples (āṭavikas). The human cost was real; the redemption arc is partially reconstructive.

entity_links.notes: Mauryan Empire at its greatest extent

sources.citation: Aśoka, Major Rock Edict XIII (primary, ~257 BCE, multiple copies — Kalsi, Shahbazgarhi, Mansehra).
Thapar, R. (1997). Aśoka and the Decline of the Mauryas. 2nd ed. Oxford UP.
Allchin, F.R. (1995). The Archaeology of Early Historic South Asia. Cambridge UP.

location_name: Alps crossing (contested pass: Col de la Traversette or Col de Clapier)
main_actor: Hannibal Barca of Carthage
description: In autumn 218 BCE Hannibal led a Carthaginian-Iberian-Numidian army of ~50,000 infantry, 9,000 cavalry, and 37 war elephants across the Alps into Roman-held northern Italy, opening the Second Punic War on Roman soil. The crossing — through snow, against hostile Gallic tribes, with heavy logistical losses — took 15 days. Polybius (eyewitness to Scipio's circle) reports roughly half the army died en route. Hannibal's subsequent Italian campaign produced the crushing Roman defeats at Trebia (-218), Trasimene (-217), and Cannae (-216), but failed to force Rome to terms. A campaign deposit of trampled soil and horse dung at the Col de la Traversette (Mahaney 2016) provides physical evidence for that pass as the likely crossing point.
casualties_source: Polybius III.56 (~half the army lost on the crossing)
ethical_notes: Roman historiography (Livy, later Silius Italicus) villainised Hannibal as the archetype of the treacherous foreign enemy; the Carthaginian side of the Punic Wars was preserved almost exclusively through hostile sources because after the destruction of Carthage (-146) the Punic-language historiography was lost. Polybius, himself a Greek hostage at Rome, wrote the most even-handed surviving account — and that is still written for Roman readers. The Carthaginian political and cultural perspective on the Second Punic War is largely irrecoverable.

sources.citation: Polybius, Histories III.34-56 (primary, ~140 BCE).
Livy, Ab Urbe Condita XXI.32-38 (primary, ~25 BCE).
Mahaney, W.C. et al. (2016). 'Biostratigraphic Evidence for the Alpine Crossing Route of Hannibal.' Archaeometry 59/1.

main_actor: Liu Bang (漢高祖) and Han Xin against Xiang Yu of Chu
description: The decisive battle of the Chu-Han contention (206-202 BCE). Liu Bang's Han forces under the general Han Xin encircled Xiang Yu's Chu army at Gaixia. Sima Qian's Shiji preserves the literary episode of the 'songs of Chu in the four quarters' — Han soldiers singing Chu songs to demoralise Xiang Yu's homesick troops. Xiang Yu broke out with a small escort, fled to the Wu River, and committed suicide. Liu Bang was proclaimed emperor (漢高祖 Han Gaozu) and founded the Han Dynasty, which would rule China (with one interregnum) for four centuries and give its name to the dominant Chinese ethnicity (漢族).
casualties_source: Sima Qian, Shiji 7 (Annals of Xiang Yu); modern estimates
ethical_notes: The Chu-Han narrative is preserved almost entirely through Sima Qian's Shiji (c. -94), which is already a literary reconstruction with explicit moral framing: Xiang Yu as the tragic aristocratic warrior, Liu Bang as the pragmatic and sometimes ruthless commoner-emperor. The literary stylisation does not undermine the main historical facts (battle location, outcome, Liu Bang's enthronement) but it does mean the details, speeches, and casualty tallies must be read with caution.

entity_links.notes: Han Dynasty begins under Gaozu (Liu Bang)

sources.citation: Sima Qian, Shiji (史記), chapters 7-8 (primary, ~94 BCE).
Ban Gu, Han Shu (漢書) chapter 1A (primary, ~92 CE).
Loewe, M. (ed.) (1986). The Cambridge History of China, Vol. 1: The Ch'in and Han Empires. Cambridge UP.

main_actor: Senatorial conspiracy led by M. Iunius Brutus and C. Cassius Longinus
description: On the Ides of March (15 March 44 BCE) a senatorial conspiracy of at least 23 men stabbed Gaius Julius Caesar to death at a Senate meeting held in the Curia of Pompey. Caesar — dictator perpetuo since February, and the object of a semi-divine cult already in his lifetime — received 23 wounds, only one of which Suetonius considered mortal. The conspirators expected the Republic to restore itself; instead the assassination triggered 13 years of further civil war, ending with Octavian's victory at Actium (-31) and the de facto foundation of the Principate (-27). The name 'Caesar' became a title for all subsequent Roman emperors.
casualties_source: Suetonius, Julius 82; Plutarch, Life of Caesar 66
ethical_notes: Caesar is one of the best-documented figures of antiquity: his own Commentarii, Cicero's contemporary letters and speeches, Asinius Pollio (lost but reused by Plutarch and Appian), and the imperial biographers all intersect. The Tyrannicide vs. Martyr framing of the assassination was already politically contested within 44-43 BCE itself (Cicero's Second Philippic; the posthumous funeral games of Caesar at which a comet appeared and was declared Caesar's apotheosis). Both readings have continued — the assassins appear on coins issued by Brutus (EID MAR denarius) advertising the murder as liberation.

entity_links.notes: Triggers final civil wars that end the Republic

sources.citation: Suetonius, De Vita Caesarum: Divus Iulius 76-82 (primary, ~120 CE).
Plutarch, Life of Caesar 62-67; Life of Brutus 14-20 (primary, ~100 CE).
Appian, Bella Civilia II.111-117 (primary, ~160 CE).
Strauss, B. (2015). The Death of Caesar: The Story of History's Most Famous Assassination. Simon & Schuster.

main_actor: Arminius of the Cherusci against P. Quinctilius Varus
description: Over three days (probably September 9 CE) a Germanic coalition led by Arminius — a Romanised Cheruscan nobleman who had served as a Roman auxiliary officer — ambushed three Roman legions (XVII, XVIII, XIX) under P. Quinctilius Varus in the forests of northwestern Germany. The entire Roman force of ~15,000-20,000 men was annihilated. Varus committed suicide; his head was sent to the Marcomannic king Maroboduus and then to Rome. Augustus is reported to have cried 'Quinctili Vare, legiones redde!' The legionary numbers XVII, XVIII, XIX were never reissued. The defeat halted Roman expansion east of the Rhine permanently; the Rhine became the imperial frontier for the next four centuries.
casualties_source: Cassius Dio LVI.18-24; archaeological data from Kalkriese
ethical_notes: Arminius was rehabilitated in 19th-century German nationalism (the Hermannsdenkmal monument, 1875) as a proto-national liberator against Rome. Tacitus's Annales I-II, the main surviving narrative, is already highly rhetorical; the archaeological identification of Kalkriese as the battlefield (Schlüter 1993 onward) has grounded the event in material evidence independent of the literary tradition. The defeat was a major structural turning point — Rome did not fail to conquer Germania for lack of will but for the military-logistical consequences of this battle.

sources.citation: Tacitus, Annales I.55-71, II.7-26 (primary, ~115 CE).
Cassius Dio, Historia Romana LVI.18-24 (primary, ~220 CE).
Wells, P.S. (2003). The Battle That Stopped Rome. Norton.

main_actor: Pontius Pilatus, Roman prefect of Judaea
description: Yeshua of Nazareth, a Galilean Jewish preacher and movement leader, was executed by Roman crucifixion under the prefect Pontius Pilate, probably on 14 or 15 Nisan (~7 April 30 CE or ~3 April 33 CE). The charge on the titulus was political: 'King of the Jews' (INRI — Iesus Nazarenus Rex Iudaeorum). Tacitus (Annales XV.44, ~115 CE) and Josephus (Antiquitates XVIII.63-64, ~94 CE — the Testimonium Flavianum, partly Christianised in transmission) independently attest the execution. The movement his followers founded — which within a century would call itself Christianoi — became, by the 4th century, the state religion of the Roman Empire and, through medieval and early modern expansion, the most numerous religion in the world.
ethical_notes: The historicity of Jesus of Nazareth as a Jewish preacher executed by Roman authority is accepted by the overwhelming majority of academic historians (Meier, Ehrman, Vermes, Fredriksen); theological claims (resurrection, divinity) are separate and lie outside historiographic method. The Christian reception of the crucifixion narrative produced two millennia of anti-Jewish violence — the 'deicide' accusation used to justify pogroms, expulsions, and the Shoah — based on a contested reading that shifts agency from Roman imperial to Jewish religious authorities. Vatican II (Nostra Aetate, 1965) formally repudiated the collective-guilt reading. AtlasPI records the event as Roman political execution of a Jewish provincial subject, which is its minimum factual core.

sources.citation: Gospels of Mark, Matthew, Luke, John (primary, ~70-100 CE).
Tacitus, Annales XV.44 (primary, ~115 CE).
Flavius Josephus, Antiquitates Iudaicae XVIII.63-64 (primary, ~94 CE, Testimonium Flavianum).
Fredriksen, P. (1999). Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews. Knopf.

main_actor: Shimon bar Kosiba ('Bar Kokhba'), Judaean rebel forces
description: The third and final Jewish revolt against Rome, triggered by the emperor Hadrian's plan to refound Jerusalem as the Roman colonia Aelia Capitolina and reportedly to ban circumcision. Shimon bar Kosiba was proclaimed Nasi of Israel and acclaimed as messiah ('Bar Kokhba', son of the star) by Rabbi Akiva. The Romans eventually deployed up to 12 legions under Sextus Iulius Severus. Cassius Dio (LXIX.14) reports 580,000 Jewish dead by the sword, not counting those lost to famine and disease; 50 fortified towns and 985 villages destroyed; Judaea 'almost completely depopulated'. After the defeat Hadrian renamed the province Syria Palaestina, barred Jews from entering Jerusalem, and executed leading rabbis (the Ten Martyrs tradition).
casualties_source: Cassius Dio LXIX.14 (580,000 Jewish war dead); modern estimates Mor (2016)
ethical_notes: The revolt and its suppression were a demographic catastrophe for Judaean Jewry and a decisive turning point in the formation of diaspora-centred rabbinic Judaism. Hadrian's renaming of the province from Iudaea to Syria Palaestina was a deliberate attempt to erase the Jewish link to the land, and the renaming itself has been repeatedly re-weaponised (British Mandate 1922-48 use of 'Palestine'; contemporary political debates). AtlasPI records both the violence of Roman suppression and the demographic reality that Jews remained present in the region (notably Galilee and later the Tiberias rabbinic centre) — the 'complete depopulation' of Dio is hyperbole.

sources.citation: Cassius Dio, Historia Romana LXIX.12-14 (primary, ~220 CE).
Bar Kokhba letters, Nahal Hever cave (primary documents, ~132-135 CE).
Mor, M. (2016). The Second Jewish Revolt: The Bar Kokhba War, 132-136 CE. Brill.

main_actor: Emperors Constantine I (West) and Licinius (East)
description: In February 313 CE the Augusti Constantine (West) and Licinius (East) agreed at Mediolanum on a policy of universal religious toleration, granting Christians — and all other cults — the free exercise of their religion and ordering the restitution of confiscated church property. The measure ended the Great Persecution begun under Diocletian in 303 CE, during which thousands of Christians had been executed, tortured, or sent to the mines. The 'Edict' is in fact preserved as two separate rescripts issued by Licinius to provincial governors (Lactantius, De Mortibus Persecutorum 48; Eusebius, Historia Ecclesiastica X.5). With the subsequent Constantinian imperial patronage of the Church (Council of Nicaea 325), the toleration became imperial privilege, and by the end of the 4th century (Edict of Thessalonica, 380) orthodox Christianity was the state religion.
ethical_notes: The 'Edict of Milan' is foundational in Christian historiography as the end of persecution, but the shift from tolerated minority to imperial orthodoxy was rapid and violent: the legal suppression of pagan sacrifice (Theodosius I, 391), the destruction of the Serapeum of Alexandria (392), the lynching of Hypatia (415), and successive anti-heretical persecutions (of Donatists, Arians, Nestorians, Monophysites) transferred the machinery of imperial coercion from anti-Christian to inter-Christian and anti-pagan use within a century. Toleration was not a stable category.

entity_links.notes: Joint issuance by Constantine and Licinius

sources.citation: Lactantius, De Mortibus Persecutorum 48 (primary, ~315 CE).
Eusebius, Historia Ecclesiastica X.5.1-14 (primary, ~324 CE).
Barnes, T.D. (1981). Constantine and Eusebius. Harvard UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

main_actor: Emperor Constantine I
description: On 11 May 330 CE Constantine I dedicated the rebuilt Greek city of Byzantion as his new imperial capital, Nova Roma — soon renamed Constantinopolis. The site on the Bosphorus commanded the crossing between Europe and Asia, the Black Sea grain trade, and the eastern frontier. The new city was endowed with a Senate, forum, hippodrome, imperial palace, and a programme of churches (Hagia Eirene, later Hagia Sophia). Its foundation was a strategic recognition that the empire's demographic and economic centre of gravity had shifted eastward. Constantinople would be the capital of the eastern Roman (later called Byzantine) empire for 1123 years, until 1453; and thereafter of the Ottoman Empire until 1922.
ethical_notes: Calling the post-330 Roman state 'Byzantine' rather than 'Roman' is a 16th-century historiographic invention (Hieronymus Wolf, 1557) that has been critiqued as orientalising (Kaldellis 2019). The inhabitants called themselves Rhomaioi and their state Basileia Rhomaion; they were Romans, and the eastern empire was continuous with the empire Augustus had consolidated in 27 BCE. AtlasPI treats the eastern empire as continuous Rome and reserves 'Byzantine' as a historiographic convention, not a substantive break.

sources.citation: Eusebius, Vita Constantini III.48-49 (primary, ~337 CE).
Sozomen, Historia Ecclesiastica II.3 (primary, ~440 CE).
Kaldellis, A. (2019). Romanland: Ethnicity and Empire in Byzantium. Harvard UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

main_actor: Gothic confederation (Thervingi and Greuthungi) under Fritigern, against emperor Valens
description: On 9 August 378 CE the eastern emperor Valens, without waiting for the western reinforcements under Gratian, engaged a Gothic army under Fritigern near Adrianople. The Gothic cavalry returned from foraging at the decisive moment, surrounded the Roman formation, and destroyed it. Valens was killed — his body never recovered — along with two-thirds of his field army, including 35 tribunes and most of the senior eastern command. Ammianus Marcellinus, whose Res Gestae ends with this battle, called it 'no battle in our history, except Cannae, was so destructive'. The defeat forced Theodosius I to settle the Goths inside imperial territory as federates (foedus 382), a precedent that reshaped the fifth-century western empire.
casualties_source: Ammianus Marcellinus XXXI.12-13 (~two-thirds of the army); modern estimates Burns (1994)
ethical_notes: The Gothic revolt of 376-378 had its immediate cause in imperial malfeasance: the Thervingi had been allowed across the Danube in 376 as refugees from Hunnic pressure, and then the Roman administrators Lupicinus and Maximus extracted bribes and sold Gothic children into slavery in exchange for dog-meat rations (Ammianus XXXI.4). Adrianople is often treated as the beginning of the 'barbarian invasions' narrative, which obscures that the Goths had tried first to be law-abiding imperial subjects.

entity_links.notes: Eastern Roman army destroyed; emperor killed

sources.citation: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae XXXI.12-13 (primary, ~390 CE).
Zosimus, Historia Nova IV.23-24 (primary, ~500 CE).
Burns, T.S. (1994). Barbarians Within the Gates of Rome. Indiana UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

main_actor: Emperors Theodosius I, Gratian, Valentinian II
description: On 27 February 380 CE the three Augusti issued the rescript 'Cunctos populos' (preserved as Codex Theodosianus XVI.1.2) requiring all peoples of the empire to hold the Nicene Christian faith (Father-Son-Holy Spirit consubstantial) taught by Bishop Damasus of Rome and Peter of Alexandria. Those who dissented were declared heretics (haereticorum dogma), denied the name of 'catholic church', and threatened with divine and imperial punishment. Subsequent legislation (391-392) outlawed pagan sacrifice, closed temples, and established the legal framework for enforced Nicene orthodoxy. Thessalonica marks the transition from toleration (Milan 313) to state religion.
ethical_notes: The Edict of Thessalonica initiated roughly fifteen centuries during which Christian imperial and royal power treated religious dissent as a political crime. The immediate casualties included pagans (Hypatia of Alexandria, 415; destruction of the Serapeum, 392), Arian Christians (Gothic and other), Donatists, Manichaeans, and later Jews (canonical anti-Jewish legislation from Justinian onward). AtlasPI records Thessalonica as the legal origin of this pattern, alongside its narrow theological substance — both are its historical significance.

sources.citation: Codex Theodosianus XVI.1.2 'Cunctos populos' (primary, 27 Feb 380 CE).
Sozomen, Historia Ecclesiastica VII.4 (primary, ~440 CE).
Cameron, A. (2011). The Last Pagans of Rome. Oxford UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

location_name: Mediolanum (administrative seat at death of Theodosius)
main_actor: Theodosius I (on his deathbed), his sons Arcadius and Honorius
description: On 17 January 395 CE Theodosius I — the last emperor to rule a united empire — died at Milan and bequeathed the eastern half (praefectura praetorio Orientis et Illyrici) to his 17-year-old son Arcadius and the western half (Italia, Galliae, Britanniae, Africa, Hispaniae) to his 10-year-old son Honorius. The administrative division had precedents (Diocletianic Tetrarchy, multiple co-Augusti) but the 395 split was permanent in practice: no single emperor would ever again rule both halves. The western empire would collapse within 81 years (476); the eastern empire would continue until 1453. Contemporary Romans did not experience 395 as dissolution — they would have said there were two Augusti, as before — but retrospectively the date is the conventional marker.
ethical_notes: Using 395 as the 'fall of unified Rome' is a retrospective historiographic convention. Both halves continued to use the shared Latin and Greek imperial-legal apparatus, issued joint consuls, and recognised each other as legitimate co-emperors. The effective divergence in fiscal health, army composition, and urban continuity widened through the 5th century. AtlasPI records 395 as the last succession of a unified Roman imperial title, while noting that the eastern empire remained fully Roman in self-understanding for another millennium.

entity_links.notes: Permanent administrative split into eastern and western halves

sources.citation: Zosimus, Historia Nova IV.59 (primary, ~500 CE).
Claudian, In Rufinum II (primary, ~397 CE).
Cameron, A. (1998). The Cambridge Ancient History XIII: The Late Empire. Cambridge UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

main_actor: Flavius Aetius (Roman) and Theodoric I (Visigoth) against Attila (Hun)
description: On 20 June 451 CE the western Roman magister militum Flavius Aetius, commanding an army that combined Roman regulars with Visigoth, Alan, Burgundian, Frankish, and Armorican federates under the Visigothic king Theodoric I, confronted Attila's Hunnic confederation (Huns, Ostrogoths, Gepids, Rugians, Heruli) on the Catalaunian Plains in north-eastern Gaul. The battle lasted into the night; Theodoric I was killed; casualties were catastrophic on both sides. Attila withdrew across the Rhine and the Hunnic threat to Gaul ended, though Attila invaded northern Italy the next year before dying in 453. The battle is the last major victory in the field for a western Roman army.
casualties_source: Jordanes, Getica XL (165,000 and 300,000 in different manuscripts — almost certainly inflated)
ethical_notes: The battle has been romanticised as a 'clash of civilisations' (West vs. steppe) since Edward Gibbon — a framing that erases that the 'Roman' side was itself majority non-Roman (Visigothic, Frankish, Alan, Armorican) and that Hunnic polities had longstanding treaty relations with Rome. It is more accurately a clash of two multi-ethnic late-antique military confederations. The 5th-century demographic upheavals across Europe produced real suffering — displaced populations, destroyed cities — but the Gibbon 'barbarians vs. civilisation' frame is itself a historiographic bias.

entity_links.notes: Western Roman field army under Aetius
Visigoths as Roman federates; king Theodoric I killed

sources.citation: Jordanes, Getica XXXVI-XL (primary, ~551 CE).
Hydatius, Chronicon (primary, ~469 CE).
Kelly, C. (2009). Attila the Hun: Barbarian Terror and the Fall of the Roman Empire. Bodley Head.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

main_actor: Odoacer, king of the Sciri and foederati of Italy
description: On 4 September 476 CE the Germanic general Odoacer, at the head of the Italian foederati, deposed the 16-year-old western emperor Romulus Augustulus ('little Augustus'), spared his life, and pensioned him to Campania. Odoacer did not claim the imperial title: he sent the western imperial regalia to Constantinople and ruled Italy as rex, acknowledging the eastern emperor Zeno as sole Augustus. The event is the conventional marker of the 'fall of the Western Roman Empire', though contemporaries did not necessarily perceive it as final — the western imperial title had been vacated and restored several times in preceding decades, and the remaining rump emperor Julius Nepos continued to claim legitimacy from Dalmatia until his murder in 480. Italy would now be ruled successively by Odoacer, Theodoric the Ostrogoth (493-526), the Byzantine reconquest (535-568), and the Lombards.
ethical_notes: The 476 date is a historiographic convention popularised by Marcellinus Comes and Jordanes in the 6th century and canonised in Gibbon's Decline and Fall. Contemporary Romans living in 476-500 would not have said 'the empire has fallen' — they would have said Italy has a new governor who acknowledges the Augustus at Constantinople. The real transformation was multi-generational: urban decline, monetisation of fiscal relations, disappearance of the western Senate, Christianisation of civic identity. Framing 476 as 'the fall' flattens that slow-motion transformation and also invisibilises the continuity of Roman identity in the East, where it lasted until 1453.

entity_links.notes: Western imperial line formally ends; eastern empire continues

sources.citation: Marcellinus Comes, Chronicon s.a. 476 (primary, ~534 CE).
Jordanes, Getica XLVI.242 (primary, ~551 CE).
Heather, P. (2006). The Fall of the Roman Empire: A New History. Oxford UP.
Ward-Perkins, B. (2005). The Fall of Rome and the End of Civilization. Oxford UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ
